summary:
In cases of iron deficiency anemia, cells respond by enhancing their ability to absorb more iron through increased production of transferrin receptors due to regulatory signals from iron-responsive elements within the nucleus. This mechanism helps maintain adequate intracellular iron stores despite reduced external availability. Key point here involves enhanced uptake via receptor modulation rather than direct storage changes or other processes not directly related to immediate absorption needs under these conditions. To ensure clarity while focusing strictly on core aspects:

Cells adapt to lower extracellular iron concentrations during iron-deficiency anemia by boosting transferrin receptor numbers at a genetic level guided by iron-responsive element signaling; this enhances internal iron acquisition necessary before significant alterations occur elsewhere such as altered ferritin content which would be secondary effects focused primarily post-absorption handling
